clinicalStem: A 35-year-old man presents with a 2 cm, slowly enlarging, smooth, round swelling on his upper back. It is non-tender, soft, and fluctuant. On close inspection there is a small central punctum. The overlying skin is normal in colour. When pressed gently, a white, cheesy, malodorous material is expressed. There are no regional lymph nodes enlarged. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: The combination of a slowly growing, smooth, fluctuant subcutaneous swelling with a central punctum and expressible white cheesy (keratinous) material is pathognomonic of an epidermal inclusion cyst. The punctum represents a blocked pilosebaceous follicle and the contents are degraded keratin, not pus. Lipomas are soft but doughy, transilluminate, and lack a punctum. Dermoid cysts are deep to skin, often at embryonic fusion lines, without a punctum. An abscess is acutely tender and erythematous.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: central punctum plus expressible cheesy keratinous contents are the diagnostic hallmarks of an epidermal inclusion cyst.
A lipoma is a benign fatty tumour — it is soft and lobulated but does not have a punctum, does not express keratinous material, and transilluminates clearly.
A dermoid cyst occurs at embryonic fusion lines (e.g., lateral eyebrow, midline neck), is deep-seated, and does not have a punctum or expressible contents at the skin surface.
An abscess presents acutely with pain, erythema, local heat, and systemic signs of infection; the contents are purulent rather than cheesy and malodorous.
A lymph node is firm to rubbery, lies along recognised lymphatic chains, moves separately from the overlying skin, and never has a punctum or expressible cheesy material.
clinicalPearl: The central punctum is the pathognomonic feature of an epidermal inclusion cyst — always look for it to distinguish from a lipoma.
keyClue: Central punctum with expressible white cheesy (keratinous) material
commonExamTrap: Calling it a lipoma — lipomas lack a punctum and do not express keratinous contents.
missedConcept: The punctum represents the blocked pilosebaceous duct; the cyst contents are keratin, not sebum.

clinicalStem: A 50-year-old woman presents with a 5 cm soft, lobulated, non-tender swelling in her right forearm. The swelling moves freely with the overlying skin and is easily compressible. It has been present for three years and is slowly increasing in size. There is no skin discolouration, no fixity to deep fascia, and no regional lymphadenopathy. She is otherwise well. Which single investigation would BEST confirm the diagnosis before surgical excision?
explanation: Ultrasound is the first-line imaging investigation for a suspected lipoma. It is readily available, inexpensive, radiation-free, and characteristically shows a well-defined, homogeneous, hyperechoic lesion parallel to the skin surface. Ultrasound can differentiate a lipoma from a liposarcoma (which shows internal septae, heterogeneity, or deep extension) and is sufficient for planning excision of benign-appearing lesions. MRI is reserved for large (>5 cm), deep, or indeterminate lesions where liposarcoma cannot be excluded on ultrasound.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MRI is the gold standard for soft tissue tumours when malignancy is suspected (e.g., >5 cm, deep to fascia, rapid growth, heterogeneous), but ultrasound is the appropriate first-line investigation for a typical small superficial lipoma.
FNAC yields adipocytes but cannot exclude liposarcoma reliably; it is not the standard investigation for a soft tissue mass of this type.
CT involves ionising radiation and lacks the soft-tissue contrast needed to characterise lipomas; it is not the first choice for a superficial forearm swelling.
Correct: ultrasound is the first-line, most cost-effective imaging for a suspected superficial lipoma and can differentiate benign from worrying features.
Core needle biopsy is invasive and reserved for lesions where imaging suggests malignancy; it is not routinely indicated for clinically typical lipomas.
clinicalPearl: Ultrasound is the first-line investigation for a suspected lipoma; request MRI if the lesion is >5 cm, deep to fascia, rapidly growing, or heterogeneous on ultrasound — features that raise concern for liposarcoma.
keyClue: Soft, lobulated, slowly growing, mobile swelling moving with overlying skin — classic lipoma
commonExamTrap: Choosing MRI as first-line — MRI is reserved for large or deep lesions where malignancy cannot be excluded; ultrasound comes first.
missedConcept: Features that distinguish a lipoma from a liposarcoma on imaging include internal septae >2 mm, heterogeneity, deep location, and size >5 cm.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old female secretary presents with a 2-month history of a smooth, round, 2 cm swelling on the dorsum of her right wrist. It appears and enlarges during typing. The swelling is transilluminant, non-tender, and feels tense. It is not attached to the overlying skin but appears attached to the underlying joint. There is no lymphadenopathy and wrist movements are only mildly restricted. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Giant cell tumour of tendon sheath
explanation: A ganglion cyst is the most common soft tissue swelling of the wrist, arising from the joint capsule or tendon sheath and filled with thick mucinous fluid. The classic features are: dorsum of the wrist, tense transilluminant swelling attached to the underlying joint rather than the overlying skin, and exacerbation with activity. Transillumination confirms the cystic nature and excludes solid tumours. Sebaceous cysts have a punctum and are attached to skin. Lipomas are non-transilluminant, softer, and not wrist-joint-associated.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: tense, transilluminant, wrist-dorsum swelling attached to joint capsule with activity-related enlargement is classic ganglion cyst.
Sebaceous cysts are attached to the overlying skin (with a punctum), not to the joint; they contain keratinous material and do not transilluminate.
A lipoma is soft, lobulated, freely mobile, non-transilluminant, and not specifically associated with a joint capsule.
Rheumatoid nodules are firm, non-transilluminant subcutaneous nodules near pressure points; they occur in seropositive RA and are not wrist-joint-specific smooth cysts.
Giant cell tumour of the tendon sheath (tenosynovial giant cell tumour) is a firm, solid, slowly growing lesion on the flexor surface of the fingers; it is non-transilluminant.
clinicalPearl: Ganglion cyst: most common wrist swelling — tense, transilluminant, attached to joint/tendon sheath, enlarges with wrist use. Aspirate or excise for treatment.
keyClue: Tense, transilluminant, round swelling on dorsum of wrist attached to underlying joint, worsened by activity
commonExamTrap: Choosing sebaceous cyst — sebaceous cysts are attached to overlying skin, have a punctum, and are not transilluminant.
missedConcept: Transillumination confirms a cystic fluid-filled lesion and is the key bedside test differentiating ganglion from solid tumours.

clinicalStem: A 58-year-old fair-skinned farmer presents with a 1.5 cm pigmented lesion on his left shoulder. He noticed it three months ago and reports that the lesion has increased in size, changed colour from brown to black with irregular patches of red, and started to bleed on minor trauma. On examination the lesion has an irregular, notched border and the pigmentation is heterogeneous (shades of brown, black, and red). There are no palpable axillary lymph nodes. Which clinical feature is the MOST important reason to suspect malignant melanoma over a benign naevus?
options: Asymmetry combined with border irregularity and multiple colours
Recent onset within the last three months
explanation: The ABCDE criteria (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Colour variation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution) are the clinical diagnostic tool for melanoma. In this case, the combination of asymmetry, an irregular notched border, and multiple colours (brown, black, red) within the same lesion is the single most diagnostically compelling feature cluster — meeting three major ABCDE criteria simultaneously. Bleeding is a late feature and size alone is not diagnostic. Sun exposure is a risk factor, not a diagnostic criterion.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: meeting three ABCDE criteria (asymmetry + border irregularity + colour variation) simultaneously is the strongest clinical indicator of malignant melanoma.
Size >6 mm (not >1 cm) is one ABCDE criterion, but size alone is insufficient to diagnose melanoma — small melanomas exist and many large benign naevi exceed 1 cm.
Occupational sun exposure is a risk factor for developing melanoma but is not a diagnostic criterion; many melanomas occur on non-sun-exposed areas.
Recent onset or rapid change (Evolution — the 'E' in ABCDE) is important but carries less diagnostic weight than the combined morphological criteria of asymmetry, border, and colour.
Bleeding on minor trauma suggests a friable lesion and raises concern, but it is a late and non-specific feature also seen in pyogenic granuloma and seborrhoeic keratosis.
clinicalPearl: Apply the ABCDE criteria to every pigmented lesion — Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Colour variation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution. Multiple positive criteria demand urgent dermatology referral.
keyClue: Asymmetry + irregular notched border + multi-colour pigmentation (brown, black, red) — three ABCDE criteria met
commonExamTrap: Fixating on bleeding as the key feature — bleeding is a late sign; the morphological ABCDE triad is diagnostically primary.
missedConcept: Colour variation (multiple shades including red, white, or blue-black within one lesion) is highly specific for melanoma and reflects heterogeneous cell differentiation.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman undergoes wide local excision of a 2.8 mm Breslow-thickness melanoma on her right calf. Histology confirms no ulceration and the mitotic rate is 1/mm². She is otherwise well with no palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy and no clinical evidence of distant metastasis. The oncology multidisciplinary team is planning her staging workup. Which single procedure is MOST appropriate for accurate locoregional staging at this point?
options: Elective complete inguinal lymph node dissection
PET-CT scan of the whole body
Ultrasound of the right inguinal region
explanation: Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is the standard staging procedure for primary melanoma with Breslow thickness ≥1.0 mm (or <1.0 mm with adverse features). At 2.8 mm Breslow thickness, SLNB accurately determines nodal status without the morbidity of complete node dissection. A positive SLNB upstages to Stage III and guides further management. Elective complete dissection is not indicated in a clinically node-negative patient. PET-CT is reserved for Stage III/IV disease. Inguinal ultrasound cannot reliably detect micrometastases.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: SLNB is the standard of care for staging melanoma ≥1 mm Breslow thickness in a clinically node-negative patient — it identifies microscopic nodal disease with minimal morbidity.

Elective complete lymph node dissection is not recommended for clinically node-negative patients; it carries significant morbidity (lymphoedema, wound complications) with no proven survival benefit over SLNB-guided management.

PET-CT is highly sensitive for distant metastasis but is indicated for Stage III or IV melanoma; in a clinically localised Stage IIB lesion, PET-CT is not the first staging step and may be false-negative for micrometastases.
CT chest-abdomen-pelvis is used for staging Stage III/IV melanoma to detect visceral metastases; it is not the appropriate next step for locoregional staging in this clinically node-negative patient.
Inguinal ultrasound can detect enlarged nodes but cannot reliably exclude micrometastatic disease; SLNB is significantly more accurate for locoregional staging.
clinicalPearl: SLNB is indicated for all melanomas ≥1 mm Breslow thickness in a clinically node-negative patient — it is the most accurate locoregional staging tool and determines eligibility for adjuvant therapy.
keyClue: 2.8 mm Breslow thickness melanoma, clinically node-negative — SLNB threshold is ≥1 mm
commonExamTrap: Ordering PET-CT for initial staging — PET-CT is for Stage III/IV; SLNB comes first for locoregional staging in node-negative patients.
missedConcept: Breslow thickness (in mm) is the single most important prognostic factor in melanoma; SLNB is the key staging procedure that determines nodal micrometastatic status.

clinicalStem: A 72-year-old retired teacher presents with a 3 cm irregularly shaped, flat, pigmented lesion on her left cheek. It has been present for over 10 years, gradually enlarging. The lesion has varied shades of tan, brown, and dark brown with areas of hypopigmentation. There is no ulceration, bleeding, or raised nodular component. She has a long history of sun exposure. Dermoscopy reveals an asymmetric follicular pigmentation pattern. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
explanation: Lentigo maligna (melanoma in situ) occurs on chronically sun-damaged skin (typically face) in elderly patients, grows very slowly over years, and appears as a flat, irregularly pigmented macular lesion with varying shades including areas of regression (hypopigmentation). Dermoscopy showing asymmetric follicular pigmentation is characteristic. It differs from solar lentigo (uniformly tan, no atypia on dermoscopy) and from superficial spreading melanoma (which tends to be faster-growing, has a raised component, and is less characteristically facial). No invasive component is present here (no raised nodule = still in situ).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: lentigo maligna is a slow-growing in-situ melanoma on chronically sun-damaged facial skin of elderly patients — asymmetric follicular pigmentation on dermoscopy is characteristic.

Solar lentigo (liver spot) is uniformly tan-brown, well-circumscribed, does not show colour variation or areas of regression, and dermoscopy shows a typical 'moth-eaten' border pattern without follicular atypia.

Superficial spreading melanoma is the most common melanoma subtype but typically grows more rapidly (months), occurs on intermittently sun-exposed skin (back, legs), often has a palpable component, and is less characteristically facial in elderly patients.
Seborrhoeic keratosis has a 'stuck-on' warty surface, is often raised, and dermoscopy shows comedo-like openings and milia cysts rather than follicular pigmentation.

Pigmented basal cell carcinoma appears as a pearly, translucent lesion with rolled borders and arborising vessels on dermoscopy; flat progressive macular pigmentation on a sun-damaged cheek is not its typical presentation.
clinicalPearl: Lentigo maligna: slowly expanding flat macular pigmentation on chronically sun-exposed facial skin of elderly patients — dermoscopy showing asymmetric follicular pigmentation is the key diagnostic finding.
keyClue: Elderly patient, sun-damaged cheek, 10-year slowly growing flat lesion with hypopigmented areas and asymmetric follicular pigmentation on dermoscopy
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing solar lentigo — the irregular colour variation, areas of regression, and dermoscopic follicular atypia distinguish lentigo maligna from a benign solar lentigo.
missedConcept: Lentigo maligna is melanoma in situ on chronically sun-damaged skin; if a raised nodule develops within it, it has progressed to lentigo maligna melanoma (invasive).

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old man of East African descent presents with a 2 cm irregularly pigmented lesion under the nail of his right great toe. He first noticed a brown streak in the nail plate 18 months ago; it has gradually widened to involve most of the nail. There is now pigmentation extending onto the proximal nail fold (Hutchinson's sign). He has no significant sun exposure history. Examination reveals no regional lymphadenopathy. What is the MOST important diagnosis to exclude?
options: Pigmented squamous cell carcinoma of the nail bed
explanation: Acral lentiginous melanoma (ALM) is the most common melanoma subtype in dark-skinned individuals and occurs on palms, soles, and subungual areas irrespective of sun exposure. A progressively widening pigmented streak with Hutchinson's sign (pigment extending onto the proximal nail fold/cuticle) is a red flag for ALM and must be excluded urgently. Subungual haematoma produces distal migration of the pigment as the nail grows and lacks Hutchinson's sign. Benign longitudinal melanonychia has a narrow, uniform streak without progression or Hutchinson's sign.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: Hutchinson's sign (proximal nail fold pigmentation) combined with a widening subungual pigmented streak in a patient of African descent is highly suspicious for acral lentiginous melanoma.
Subungual haematoma is usually post-traumatic, dark red to black, migrates distally with nail growth, and does not produce Hutchinson's sign extending onto the proximal nail fold.
Onychomycosis causes white, yellow, or brown nail discolouration with onycholysis and subungual debris; it does not produce a pigmented streak or Hutchinson's sign.
Benign longitudinal melanonychia presents as a narrow, uniform, stable, light-brown streak typically in isolation; widening, darkening, and Hutchinson's sign are not features of benign melanonychia.

Pigmented squamous cell carcinoma of the nail bed is rare and presents as a destructive, hyperkeratotic, ulcerated lesion rather than a progressively widening pigmented streak with nail fold involvement.
clinicalPearl: Hutchinson's sign (pigment extending onto the proximal nail fold) is a hallmark of acral lentiginous melanoma and demands urgent nail unit biopsy — do not mistake it for a benign melanonychia.
keyClue: Widening pigmented nail streak with Hutchinson's sign in a dark-skinned patient
commonExamTrap: Reassigning to subungual haematoma — haematoma migrates distally as the nail grows; Hutchinson's sign is never present.
missedConcept: Acral lentiginous melanoma is not related to UV exposure and is therefore the dominant melanoma subtype in people with dark skin; presentation under nails is a common exam scenario.

topic: Cleft lip and palate — classification and diagnosis
clinicalStem: A newborn male infant is noted at birth to have a visible defect in the upper lip extending into the left nostril floor, with apparent continuity through to the hard and soft palate. The infant has immediate feeding difficulties with nasal regurgitation of milk. Examination confirms the lip cleft extends to the left alveolar ridge and the palatal cleft involves both the hard and soft palates. There are no other dysmorphic features. What is the correct classification of this anomaly?
options: Complete unilateral cleft lip and palate
Incomplete bilateral cleft lip with cleft palate
explanation: A complete cleft lip extends from the vermilion through the entire lip to the nostril floor and into the alveolar ridge. When this is combined with a cleft of both the hard and soft palate, the diagnosis is complete unilateral cleft lip and palate (CLP) — affecting one side only. Feeding difficulty with nasal regurgitation occurs because the palatal cleft prevents the baby from generating suction. Incomplete clefts stop short of the nostril or alveolus. Bilateral clefts affect both sides. Submucous cleft has intact mucosa over the palate.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: cleft extends from lip through alveolus to hard and soft palate on one side — this is by definition a complete unilateral cleft lip and palate.
Bilateral cleft lip involves both sides of the lip; only the left side is involved here.
Isolated cleft palate spares the lip entirely; here the lip and alveolus are clearly involved.
Submucous cleft palate has intact overlying mucosa with a bifid uvula and midline palatal notch; it is not visible at inspection as an open gap.
Incomplete unilateral cleft lip does not reach the nostril floor or alveolus and does not involve the palate; the vignette clearly shows palatal involvement.
clinicalPearl: Complete CLP (lip + alveolus + hard and soft palate) causes immediate feeding problems due to inability to generate negative suction pressure — specialised feeding teats and early team referral are essential.
keyClue: Cleft extends through lip, alveolus, hard palate, and soft palate on one side only
commonExamTrap: Selecting bilateral cleft — bilateral means both sides of the lip are cleft; only the left side is involved here.
missedConcept: The Veau classification: Group I = soft palate only; Group II = hard + soft palate; Group III = unilateral CLP; Group IV = bilateral CLP.

topic: Cleft palate — investigation for associated anomalies
clinicalStem: A 4-day-old female infant is found to have an isolated cleft of the hard and soft palate without cleft lip. She is feeding poorly and has mild micrognathia and mild glossoptosis observed on examination. She appears to have intermittent upper airway obstruction in the supine position, which improves when she is placed prone. No cardiac murmur is heard. Which investigation should be performed FIRST to screen for a recognised syndrome associated with these combined features?
options: Genetic testing / chromosomal microarray for 22q11.2 deletion or Pierre Robin sequence workup
CT scan of the head and neck
explanation: Isolated cleft palate + micrognathia + glossoptosis is the triad of Pierre Robin sequence (PRS). PRS is associated with several syndromic diagnoses, most importantly 22q11.2 deletion syndrome (DiGeorge), Stickler syndrome, and velocardiofacial syndrome. Chromosomal microarray or targeted FISH for 22q11.2 is the recommended first genetic investigation. While echocardiography is important because conotruncal cardiac defects accompany 22q11.2, the question asks what to perform FIRST to screen for the syndrome — genetic characterisation directs all downstream management. Early syndrome identification determines prognosis and guides multidisciplinary care.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: the clinical triad of cleft palate + micrognathia + glossoptosis defines Pierre Robin sequence, which demands genetic/chromosomal testing as the first step to identify the underlying syndrome.

Echocardiogram is essential in syndromic cleft palate (especially 22q11.2) due to conotruncal defect risk but follows genetic diagnosis; it is not the first-step investigation to establish syndromic aetiology.
CT head and neck may be useful for airway assessment in severe PRS but is not the first screening investigation for the underlying syndrome.
Renal ultrasound is important when craniofacial anomalies raise concern for CHARGE syndrome or branchio-oto-renal syndrome, but the triad here points to PRS requiring genetic diagnosis first.
Chest X-ray provides general assessment but does not identify the underlying syndrome in PRS; it is not the priority first investigation.
clinicalPearl: Pierre Robin sequence (cleft palate + micrognathia + glossoptosis) demands urgent genetic testing (chromosomal microarray, 22q11.2 FISH) plus prone positioning and airway monitoring — syndromic diagnosis drives the whole management plan.
keyClue: Isolated cleft palate + micrognathia + glossoptosis with positional airway obstruction = Pierre Robin sequence
commonExamTrap: Choosing echocardiogram first — cardiac investigation is important but comes after establishing the syndromic diagnosis with genetics.
missedConcept: Pierre Robin sequence is not a syndrome itself but a sequence of anomalies; the underlying genetic cause determines prognosis and determines which other organ systems need screening.

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old boy is referred by his speech therapist for hypernasal speech and nasal air escape during articulation. His parents report frequent ear infections and the child has a 30 dB bilateral conductive hearing loss on audiometry. On inspection the oral mucosa is intact with no visible gap in the palate. However, palpation reveals a midline notch at the posterior border of the hard palate, and the uvula is bifid. There is no submandibular lymphadenopathy. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Open hard palate cleft missed at birth
explanation: Submucous cleft palate (SMCP) is characterised by the classic triad: (1) bifid uvula, (2) midline palatal notch (zona pellucida or posterior bony notch palpable), and (3) hypernasality due to velopharyngeal insufficiency. Because the overlying mucosa is intact, it is routinely missed at birth and presents later with hypernasal speech and conductive hearing loss (from associated Eustachian tube dysfunction leading to recurrent otitis media with effusion). The intact mucosa excludes an open cleft palate.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: bifid uvula + midline hard palate notch on palpation + hypernasal speech + conductive hearing loss in a child with no visible palatal gap = classic submucous cleft palate triad.

Velopharyngeal insufficiency (VPI) without structural cleft does occur but does not explain the bifid uvula and palpable bony palatal notch — those structural signs confirm SMCP as the underlying cause of the VPI.
Adenoidal hypertrophy causes hyponasal (not hypernasal) speech and nasal obstruction; it does not cause bifid uvula or palatal bony notch.
An open hard palate cleft would be visible on oral inspection — it cannot be 'intact mucosa' by definition; SMCP is specifically a mucosa-covered defect.
Palatal neuromuscular disorder (e.g., velopharyngeal dysfunction from neurological cause) can cause hypernasality but does not produce bifid uvula or a palpable bony posterior palatal notch.
clinicalPearl: Submucous cleft palate triad: bifid uvula + midline posterior palatal notch (palpable) + hypernasal speech. Intact mucosa means it is missed at birth — suspect it in any child with hypernasal speech and recurrent OME.
keyClue: Intact palatal mucosa + bifid uvula + palpable midline bony notch + hypernasal speech = submucous cleft palate
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing VPI without a structural cause — the bifid uvula and palpable bony notch are structural signs that clinch SMCP as the aetiology.
missedConcept: SMCP is the most commonly missed cleft variant because the mucosa is intact at routine inspection; palpation of the posterior palate and assessment of the uvula are mandatory.

clinicalStem: A 40-year-old man sustained a degloving injury to the dorsum of his left hand in a factory accident. After thorough wound debridement, the orthopaedic surgeon finds that the extensor tendons are exposed over a 6 × 4 cm area. The wound bed is clean with visible granulation tissue around the tendons but the tendons themselves are devoid of paratenon. A plastic surgeon is asked to recommend the best method to achieve definitive wound cover. Which option provides the BEST durable coverage for the exposed tendons in this scenario?
explanation: Exposed tendons without paratenon cannot support a skin graft (neither split- nor full-thickness) because the tendon surface lacks the vascular granulation tissue needed for graft take. The gold standard for covering exposed avascular structures (tendons, bone without periosteum, joints, neurovascular bundles) is flap reconstruction, which brings its own blood supply. A pedicled local flap (e.g., radial forearm flap) or free flap is therefore required. NPWT alone cannot permanently close a wound over exposed tendons and should only be used as a bridge.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: exposed tendons without paratenon are avascular and will not support skin graft survival; a flap (with its own blood supply) is mandatory for durable coverage.
A split-thickness skin graft relies on the wound bed for revascularisation (plasmatic imbibition then inosculation); it cannot take on bare avascular tendon lacking paratenon — it will fail.
A full-thickness skin graft similarly requires a well-vascularised granulating wound bed; it has higher metabolic demands than STSG and will fail even more reliably on avascular tendon.
NPWT promotes granulation tissue and can be used as a bridge over tendons with intact paratenon, but it does not provide definitive coverage and cannot revascularise bare avascular tendon.
Primary closure with tension creates further ischaemia, risks dehiscence, and is contraindicated over a large tissue deficit — it does not solve the problem of avascular tendon exposure.
clinicalPearl: Exposed bone without periosteum, tendons without paratenon, or cartilage without perichondrium cannot support skin grafts — flap reconstruction is mandatory because it brings its own vascularity.
keyClue: Tendons exposed without paratenon — avascular bed that cannot support any skin graft
commonExamTrap: Choosing FTSG thinking it is superior to STSG — both fail on avascular tendon; only a flap brings sufficient blood supply.
missedConcept: The 'reconstructive ladder' — grafts require a vascularised bed; flaps are used when the recipient bed is avascular or when structural durability is needed.

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old woman of Afro-Caribbean descent develops a firm, raised, itchy, dark-red scar six months after a minor ear-piercing. The scar has grown beyond the original wound margins and now extends 1.5 cm lateral to the piercing site. She had a similar scar after a childhood appendicectomy. Her sister also has a similar lesion after a piercing. Examination confirms a firm, raised plaque extending beyond the wound boundary. Which is the correct diagnosis?
explanation: Keloid scars extend beyond the original wound boundary (clawing beyond wound margins) — this is the defining distinguishing feature from hypertrophic scars, which remain within wound margins. Additional features of keloid are: darker skin predisposition (Afro-Caribbean), predilection for earlobes, shoulders, and chest, family history, tendency to recur after excision, and symptom of pruritus and pain. Hypertrophic scars are confined to the wound and typically regress over time. The scar here clearly transgresses the wound boundary, confirming keloid.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct: scar extending beyond the original wound margin, in an Afro-Caribbean patient with family history and a typical site (earlobe), is diagnostic of keloid.

Hypertrophic scars are raised, erythematous, and firm but remain strictly within the original wound boundary; they tend to soften and flatten over months. This scar has spread 1.5 cm beyond the wound — that excludes hypertrophic scar.
Dermatofibroma is a benign dermal nodule on limbs, dimples inward on lateral compression (Fitzpatrick sign), is not associated with scarring, and does not occur at piercing sites.
Nodular fasciitis is a rapidly growing, self-limiting reactive myofibroblastic proliferation in deep soft tissue; it does not arise from a superficial wound or have a familial pattern.
Pyogenic granuloma is a rapidly growing vascular lesion that bleeds easily on touch; it is soft, friable, and pedunculated — not a firm raised plaque with a scar history.
clinicalPearl: The single key difference between keloid and hypertrophic scar is growth beyond the original wound margins — keloid transgresses; hypertrophic scar does not.
keyClue: Scar growing 1.5 cm beyond the original wound margin in an Afro-Caribbean patient with a family history = keloid
commonExamTrap: Calling it a hypertrophic scar — the scar has grown BEYOND wound margins; this is the defining feature of keloid that excludes hypertrophic scar.
missedConcept: Keloids are more common in dark-skinned individuals, have a familial tendency, and commonly occur on earlobes, shoulders, sternum, and upper back; they do not regress spontaneously.
